coredata.dc:title: Multi-array wax paper-based platform for the colorimetric determination of metal ions in human biofluids: Smart wearable optical sensor (SWOS) towards bioanalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bahavarnia, Farnaz
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Metal ions are needed to keep the human body healthy, as their presence has or can affect vital biological functions in humans and their existence is essential for survival. Current methods for metal ion analysis struggle with challenges such as low sensitivity, lack of selectivity and complex procedures. Therefore, clinicians urgently need an efficient analysis method/technique. In the present study, a new chemosensing method was proposed for the sensitive recognition of Co(II), Cu (II), and Pb (II) ions. In this method, a chemical reaction occurs between metal ions and triangular silver nanoparticles (TA-AgNPs) which served as optical prob, resulting in a color change detected by an engineered colorimetric method. UV–visible spectrophotometry also confirms the reaction, as the interaction between metal ions and TA-AgNPs causes a significant change in the absorption spectrum. This enables the rapid and reliable measurement of these important metal ions with a detection limit of less than 10 nM to 300 mM in human body fluids. Finally, Co(II), Cu (II), and Pb (II) cations were determined by a novel microfluidic chemosensor which engineered by multi-array wax paper-based method. Therefore, a novel portable photo-sensor was developed for the sensitive and specific monitoring of Co(II), Cu (II), and Pb (II) cations in human real samples. In the presence of metal ions, constructed microfluidic paper-based colorimetric devices (μPCDs) work based on color alternation of the sensing probe, such that the blue color of the TA-AgNPrs solution was changed to light orange in the presence of Co(II), and Cu(II) was changed to yellow, Pb(II) was changed to light blue which confirmed suitable application of the engineered platform for the rapid identification of ions. Therefore, an innovative method was suggested for the in-situ and on-demand opto-analysis of metal ions in human urine samples which is expected to help improve environmental health and safety in the workplace.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical chemosensing
Microfluidic
Colorimetric sensor
Silver nanoparticles
Lab-on-paper
metal ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and spectrophotometric detection of metal ions
Bioanalysis of metal ions using μPCDs
Selective identification of Cu(II) by TA-AgNPs on the surface of μPCDs
Selective identification of Co(II) by TA-AgNPs on the surface of μPCDs
Selective identification of Pb(II)by TA-AgNPs, using μPCDs
Identification of Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions in the biological sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–D) FESEM, TEM, EDAX, and DLS of AgNPrs, respectively.

Colorimetric (A & B) and spectrophotometric (C & D) detection of TA-AgNPrs, TA-AgNPrs/Ni(II), TA-AgNPrs/Pb (II), TA-AgNPrs/Cd(II), TA-AgNPrs/Fe(II), TA-AgNPrs/Mn(II), TA-AgNPrs/Cu(II), TA-AgNPrs/Co(II) in a 1:1 V/V ratio at two reaction times of 0 and 60 min, respectively.
(A, B) Histogram of peak absorption versus type of ion in two reaction times (0 and 60 min) of optical probe (TA-AgNPs) and target ions.

Spectrophotometric (A & B) and histogram of peak absorption (C & D) versus type of solution [urine, TA-AgNPrs, TA-AgNPrs/urine and TA-AgNPrs/urine/Pb (II), Co (II) Cu (II) ions] in a 1:1 V/V ratio and 1.0.5.0.5 V/V/V ratio at two reaction times of 0 and 60 min, respectively.

(A & B) High-resolution images of µPCDs modified by (1) TA-AgNPrs, (2) TA-AgNPrs/ Ni(II), (3) TA-AgNPrs/Pb(II), (4) TA-AgNPrs/Cd(II), (5) TA-AgNPrs/Fe(II), (6) TA-AgNPrs/Mn(II), (7) TA-AgNPrs/Cu(II), (8) TA-AgNPrs/Co(II) in two reaction times of 0 and 60 min, respectively.

(A & B) High-resolution images of µPCDs test for TA-AgNPrs/Urine /metal ions, (1) Urine, (2) Urine/Pb(II), (3) Urine/Co(II), (4) Urine/Cu(II), (5) TA-AgNPrs, (6) TA-AgNPrs/Urine, (7) TA-AgNPrs/Urine/Pb(II), (8) TA-AgNPrs/Urine/Co(II), (9) TA-AgNPrs/Urine/Cu(II) in two reaction times of 30, 60 min, respectively.

Histogram of peak absorption versus detection of metal ions solution throughout 60 min of reaction time with different concentrations (10 µM, 100 µM, and 1 mM) and at three reaction times 20, 40, and 60 min (1:1 v/v ratio) for three different concentrations of analytes (ions).
Comparison of various analytical methods for the determination of metal ions.

head.ce:title: Multi-array wax paper-based platform for the colorimetric determination of metal ions in human biofluids: Smart wearable optical sensor (SWOS) towards bioanalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nutrition Research Center Tabriz University of Medical Sciences Tabriz Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nutrition Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center Tabriz University of Medical Sciences Tabriz Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal ions are needed to keep the human body healthy, as their presence has or can affect vital biological functions in humans and their existence is essential for survival. Current methods for metal ion analysis struggle with challenges such as low sensitivity, lack of selectivity and complex procedures. Therefore, clinicians urgently need an efficient analysis method/technique. In the present study, a new chemosensing method was proposed for the sensitive recognition of Co(II), Cu (II), and Pb (II) ions. In this method, a chemical reaction occurs between metal ions and triangular silver nanoparticles (TA-AgNPs) which served as optical prob, resulting in a color change detected by an engineered colorimetric method. UV–visible spectrophotometry also confirms the reaction, as the interaction between metal ions and TA-AgNPs causes a significant change in the absorption spectrum. This enables the rapid and reliable measurement of these important metal ions with a detection limit of less than 10 nM to 300 mM in human body fluids. Finally, Co(II), Cu (II), and Pb (II) cations were determined by a novel microfluidic chemosensor which engineered by multi-array wax paper-based method. Therefore, a novel portable photo-sensor was developed for the sensitive and specific monitoring of Co(II), Cu (II), and Pb (II) cations in human real samples. In the presence of metal ions, constructed microfluidic paper-based colorimetric devices (μPCDs) work based on color alternation of the sensing probe, such that the blue color of the TA-AgNPrs solution was changed to light orange in the presence of Co(II), and Cu(II) was changed to yellow, Pb(II) was changed to light blue which confirmed suitable application of the engineered platform for the rapid identification of ions. Therefore, an innovative method was suggested for the in-situ and on-demand opto-analysis of metal ions in human urine samples which is expected to help improve environmental health and safety in the workplace.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Mineral metal ions are crucial for supporting the reactions that maintain homeostasis and bodily functions. The proper regulation of the neurodegenerative system directly involves essential metal ions such as Na(I), K(I), Mg(II), Ca(II), Fe(II), Cu(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Cr(II), and Mn(II). In contrast, toxic metals like Cd(II), Pb(II), and Hg(II) interfere with homeostasis and can cause disease [1–3]. Due to US EPA, the maximum contaminant level (MCL) of Pb (II), Cu(II), Co(II) ions have been set at a very low level of 0.015, 1.3, 0.05 mg/L, respectively, whereas the WHO limits it at 0.05, 1.5, 0.01 mg/L, respectively [1–5]. These harmful metal ions can alter DNA structure by reacting with nucleic bases and interacting electrostatically with phosphate groups, triggering single or double-strand breaks. The buildup of these flaws compromises the body's defense systems, prompts mutations, and can result in serious diseases [4]. Substantiated elements at low concentrations? Critically enable health, but they're hazardous in large quantities. Furthermore, heavy metal ions are significant in industry, agriculture, and papermaking, as well as possessing notable importance in medicine and nutrition and should not be overlooked [5,6].

Heavy metal ions rank among the most hazardous contaminants in both industrial settings and the environment. Due to their extensive presence, long persistence, ability to bioaccumulate, and intense toxicity even at trace levels, they pose severe risks to natural ecosystems and human health. [7–12]. Of particular concern is the effect abnormal metal ion concentrations have on bodily fluids, creating notable healthcare challenges in low-income nations. [7–10,12,13]. The development of rapid and precise methods for detecting trace amounts of heavy metal ions in environmental, industrial, and biological samples is crucial. Research indicates several analytical methods and instruments have been employed for this purpose, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, visible spectrophotometry, X-ray fluorescence, neutron activation analysis, cold vapor atomic fluorescence, and inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy. [7,14] While these conventional techniques offer high selectivity and sensitivity to determine the quantities of metal ions, they are typically expensive, require advanced instruments and expertise, entail complex preparatory procedures, and are time-intensive. Therefore, there is a preference for efficient, cost-effective sensors that can identify and measure metal ions swiftly and efficiently in environmental and industrial contexts. [15–17].

Efficient sensors have become a valuable alternative in metal analysis methodologies due to their rapid analysis capabilities, high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, ease of use, and ability to conduct on-site monitoring. These sensors are essential for detecting and quantifying trace amounts of heavy metals in environmental samples and industrial scenarios. [15–17] Concurrently, the implementation of chemical sensors is increasingly prevalent and advanced as sensor technology in metal ion analysis sees continuous growth. This expansion is closely tied to the advancements in electronic and optical technologies, aligning them with the principles of green chemistry. [18] Recently, the development of chemical sensors has progressed notably within the framework of green and sustainable chemistry, receiving widespread support from the scientific community, as it aims to reduce adverse impacts on human health and the environment. [19,20] To sum up, existing methods for analyzing metal ions face hurdles such as limited sensitivity, inadequate selectivity, and complicated processes, highlighting the pressing need for an efficient analysis technique or approach. [16–20].

Optical sensors, featuring fluorescence or colorimetric and based on nanomaterials and polymers with high selectivity and sensitivity, are ideal for detecting heavy metal ions at low concentrations. These sensors are highly regarded due to their rapid detection, straightforward protocols, suitability for in-situ sampling, portability, and cost efficiency. These advantages, combined with their unique optical properties, make them a practical and economic method for analysis. [21–23] Among these sensors, colorimetric assays are particularly popular due to benefits such as ease of transport and the ability to visually detect metal ions without the need for complex equipment.

There is a growing demand for cost-effective and portable colorimetric devices as alternatives to traditional instruments for the quick and inexpensive detection of metal ions in various human bio-samples. [24,25] Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) and microfluidic paper-based colorimetric devices (µPCDs) have recently gained significant interest for diverse bioanalytical diagnostics and sensory systems. Generally, the creation of µPCDs involves establishing hydrophobic barriers on the substrate to delineate liquid flow areas. [26] The paper's inherent hydrophilic properties facilitate lateral flow, enabling liquid transport within the porous structure. This study introduces μPCDs that eliminate the need for color-modifier reagents [27–29].

In the current study, we introduce an innovative colorimetric sensor for the precise detection of Co(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II) ions. This method involves a chemical interaction between the metal ions and triangular silver nanoparticles (TA-AgNPs), serving as optical sensors, which prompts a detectable color variation via an advanced colorimetric technique [28]. UV–visible spectrophotometry further validates this reaction, showing a notable shift in the absorption spectrum due to the binding of metal ions with TA-AgNPs. Notably, a novel paper-based chemosensor, crafted with a multi-array wax paper method, facilitates the detection of Co(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II) cations. Consequently, a new portable photo-sensor was devised for the accurate and specific surveillance of these cations in human samples. When metal ions are present, the developed microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (µPCDs) operate through a change in color of the sensing probe [21–23]. Thus, we propose a novel solution for on-the-spot and real-time optical analysis of metal ions in human urine, which is anticipated to enhance environmental health and safety in work settings.

Metal ions include (NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, NaI, FeCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, Na2CO3, CuCl2, CoCl2, Cu(SO3)2, Silver chloride (AgCl), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 96 %), Trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 by weight), and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) K-30 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Canada. Deionized water was provided by Ghazi Pharmaceutical Company, Tabriz, Iran. Glass fiber sheets were purchased from Whatman Co., Germany.

Size distribution analysis and zeta potential detection for the AgNPs produced was performed using the Zetasizer Ver from Malvern Instrument, Ltd (England) with reference code MAL1032660. The spectrophotometric studies were carried out using Shimadzu UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The digital photos were taken with a Motorola Moto G5 Plus XT smartphone equipped with a 12 MP rear camera with dual autofocus and an Android 6.0 operating system. In addition, a Samsung Galaxy A5 with a 13 MP camera was used to take the pictures. To better capture color changes in the images taken with the smartphone, a white polymer cylinder was selected. To control the brightness and avoid interference from external radiation, a circular box made of black PLA with an inner diameter of 8.0 cm and a height of 10 cm was used. The smartphone was positioned approximately 10 cm above the spot tests in the box and the images were taken with autofocus using the free Photo Metrix and Color Picker application. Additionally, a transmission electron microscope (TEM) from Adelaide, Australia, operating at 70 kV, was used to investigate the synthesis mechanism.
The TA-AgNPrs were synthesized as previously described in our earlier report with minor modification.

The surface morphology of the nanoparticles was examined using high-resolution field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi-Su8020, Czech Republic) at a working voltage of 3 kV. Their chemical composition was evaluated through energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) integrated with the FE-SEM system. The SEM images of Ag NPrs presented in Fig. 1 A exhibit a uniform triangular morphology. Meanwhile, the EDS spectrum of the synthesized nanoparticles confirms the presence of C, N, O, Na, and Ag elements in their composition (Fig. 1B). The TEM images provided in Fig. 1C reveal the presence of triangular nanoparticles with particle size of 48 nm. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was utilized to assess both the particle size distribution and surface charge. As depicted in Fig. 1D, the average size of an individual nanoparticle is 43.27 nm which is in accordance to TEM result. These measurements confirmed the successful synthesis of silver nanoprism. Investigating the zeta potential is crucial for understanding and regulating the properties of these solutions.

Urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers. Aqueous standard solutions of analyte were added into 2.0 mL of urine samples and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 8 min. The supernatant was transferred to a 2 ml vial for spectrophotometric analysis. In this work, urine samples were untreated and diluted only with distilled water in a 1:0.5 V:V ratios.

In this study, glass fiber paper and thin layer chromatography (TLC) were used to evaluate the efficacy of the papers. The results showed that glass fiber paper experiences less abrasion due to its better flow characteristics. Paraffin wax can serve as a means of identifying the target through the interaction between the excited product, which is driven by a force, and a chromogen in the detection zone. Paraffin was chosen because it has a low melting point and is thermoplastic. In addition, Paraffin, a major component of solids, is commonly used in the manufacture of µPCDs through the wax printing process and is highly resistant to most chemical compounds used in the assay. Conventional µPCDs were fabricated by the wax printing method [30,31]. The proposed pattern includes thirty-two hydrophilic circular areas and also the type of the other pattern consists of eight hydrophilic circular zones. The prepared paper template has various microchannels that have a low capillary effect and through which the liquid can easily flow. The paper template has microchannels with minimal capillarity, which ensure a smooth flow of the liquid, and designed micro-zones facilitate the simultaneous analysis of multiple samples [32].

The template is heated to 90 °C and the appropriate paper is then placed in it for 30 s. After drying, the iron sample is heated to 150 °C for two minutes. A piece of paper is placed between the iron sample and the magnet. This allows the paraffin to penetrate the structure of the paper and form hydrophilic channels on the surface. Metal ions were detected using µPCDs modified with optical nanoprobes (TA-AgNPs). Consequently, the proposed method represents progress towards the development of metal ions diagnostic kits that offer superior qualitative performance and facilitate rapid and cost-effective color changes. The ability of paraffin to penetrate the paper's structure and create a hydrophilic layer on its surface is the driving force behind this advancement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and spectrophotometric detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesized nanoprobes (TA-AgNPs) were mixed with 20 mM concentrations of various metal ions [Ni(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Fe(II), Mn(II), Cu(II), Co(II)] in a 1:1 vol ratio (V/V) and incubated for different durations (0 and 60 min) in the dark. Subsequently, both colorimetric and spectrophotometric assessments were carried out using naked-eye and UV–Vis spectrophotometric methods (Fig. 2 ). Upon the introduction of metal ions, referred to as the target analyte, into the TA-AgNPs solution, the initial blue tint of the nanoprobe shifted to yellow and orange, noticeable to the naked eye. Consequently, spectrophotometric testing was performed to confirmed obtained results in colorimetric study. Significant changes were observed in the absorption peaks of the nanoprobe with the addition of Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II), as other ions such as [Ni(II), Cd(II), Fe(II), and Mn(II)] did not significantly affect the color of the sensing probe. Thus, the chemosensor demonstrates sensitivity to Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions. The analysis of the absorption band using histogram graphs, as shown in Fig. 2(A & B) at 0 and 60 min of reaction time, indicates that maximum absorption for Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions occurs after 60 min of incubation.

TA-AgNPs possess a remarkable degree of anisotropy and showcase distinctive Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) characteristics with a high extinction coefficient. When Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions is introduced, the structure of these triangular TA-AgNPrs transforms into circular nanodisks, causing the color to transition from blue to yellow, influenced by the extent of etching. Furthermore, the UV–Vis spectra of the triangular TA-AgNPrs feature broad characteristics exhibiting three distinct bands at 335 nm, 475 nm, and 750 nm; these correspond to the out-of-plane quadrupole, in-plane quadrupole, and in-plane dipole plasmon resonances, respectively. The in-plane dipole resonance at 750 nm is particularly sensitive to the thickness and the edge length of the triangular TA-AgNPs. As anticipated, the broad 750 nm peak related to in-plane dipole resonance vanishes following Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions addition, while the peak intensity at 480–500 nm progressively rises (Fig. 3 ). These findings indicate that Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions detection with this triangular TA-AgNPs sample highly hinges on the nanoprism shape relative to the in-plane dipole resonance peak's position.

These findings were confirmed by DLS/Zeta analysis of the probe following its interaction with the metal ions, as depicted in Fig. S1 (see Supporting information). Noticeable peaks emerged at 432 nm, 446 nm, and 448 nm for Cu (II), Co (II), and Pb (II) ions after 60 min, as illustrated in Fig. S1 (refer to Supporting information). To delve deeper, the zeta potential (Zp) of the optical probe was monitored under different scenarios, including situations with and without measurements. Initially, the Zp of TA-AgNPs (the optical probe) measured 14.6 mV, with conductivity at 13.8 mS cm−1. Post interaction with metal ions, especially Pb (II), the Zp for Pb (II) as the analyte was recorded at ≤11.6 mV, while Co (II) showed ≤15.3 mV. Cu (II), on the other hand, had a Zp of ≤8.85 mV, indicating an uptick. These outcomes confirmed the connectivity between metal ions and optical probes, underscoring the proposed method’s efficacy in identifying targets. The reaction kinetics are extremely quick, finalizing within merely a minute of mixing the probe and target molecule. Beyond that, the DLS analysis identified the average particle size of TA-AgNPs as 68.80 nm (Fig. S1, (see Supporting information)). The synthesized TA-AgNPs solution appeared navy-blue with a spectrophotometric absorption peak observed at 457 nm. Additionally, TA-AgNPs displayed UV/visible emission spanning the 400 to 600 nm range. The UV–visible spectrum revealed that TA-AgNPs have an absorption peak around 457 nm. Upon contact with metal ions, the solution’s color rapidly shifted to yellow, highlighting that increasing metal ion concentration results in greater TA-AgNPs particle aggregation, leading to a pronounced color change.

The reported colorimetric and spectrophotometric methods were also employed to identify metal ions (Cu(II), Co(II), Pb(II)) in human urine samples. Based on the gathered results, Fig. 4 indicates that metal ions in the urine sample were combined with TA-AgNPrs in a 1:1 v/v ratio and a 1:0.5:0.5 V/V/V ratio. These mixtures were then analyzed using colorimetric and UV/Vis techniques. As depicted in Fig. 4 (A,B), the color of the mixture turned indigo after 60 min. Therefore, the nanoprobe has demonstrated its capability to detect metal ions in urine samples. Fig. 4 (C, D) shows the histogram of peak absorption versus the detection of urine, TA-AgNPrs, and metal ions (Cu(II), Co(II), Pb(II)) confirming the selectivity of the proposed nanoprobes for detecting Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions within 0.60 min. But, Using, UV–Vis method, we could not determine these metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioanalysis of metal ions using μPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Obtained results of spectrophotometric method (Figs. S2-S4, see Supporting information) show that there is no relation between Abs (a.u) and concentration of candidate ions. So, we conducted bioanalysis of metal ions using μPCDs. We created a testing device to evaluate our µPCDs-based detection system through economical and flexible wax molding technology, eliminating the need for a clean room or specialized equipment. The experiments were conducted on paper substrates. The device includes 8 zones, each assigned to a particular sample for analyzing the effectiveness in colorimetric detection of metal ions such as Ni(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Fe(II), Mn(II), Cu(II), and Co(II). Solutions containing 20 mM concentrations of these seven metal ions were prepared and combined with the TA-AgNPrs probe solution in a 1:1 vol ratio to prompt a distinct color change for assessment. In Fig. 4(A & B) and the Supporting video (see Supporting information), the color change initiates swiftly, with the TA-AgNPrs showing an evident change after 60 min. Remarkably, when metal ions like Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) were introduced, the solution's color changed from sea blue to orange, eventually settling to yellow after an hour (see Fig. 5 and Supporting video).

In summary, it can be inferred that the utilization of the TA-AgNPs on the structure of µPCDs have the potential to revolutionize ion discovery and present new opportunities in the field of biomedical analysis.

To further explore our research methods and gain a deeper understanding of µPCD research technology, our simple, efficient and flexible tools have been developed, which do not require a sterile environment or special equipment. The evaluation is done on paper. The system has 32 zones, each assigned to a different model. According to Lambert's law, the absorption of light by any solution remains constant, regardless of the intensity of the light output. Beer's law and Lambert's law establish a relationship between the dissolution and absorption of light. In general, the concentration of a substance is determined such that the absorbance is linearly related to the concentration. The amount of light absorbed, or the absorbance, A, is defined as:
Abs = - l o g
I /
I 0
where I0 is the initial light intensity, it is the intensity of transmitted light, and A is the absorption amount of the substance, which is defined as follows:
A = ε b c
where A is the absorbance, ε is the molar attenuation coefficient or absorptivity of the attenuating species, b is the optical path length, and c is the concentration of the attenuating species.

I0 and I are the signal intensity values of the blue, green or red channels for the blank and the sample, respectively. This demonstration outlines the process of carrying out absorbance measurements and conducting Beer's Law analysis using a simple procedure.

The experiment utilizes a cell phone application named the Photo Metrix analyzer and Color Picker analyzer, which can instantly calculate the average RGB value of images captured in real-time within the camera view, as the light detector [42]. The experiment involves using either light reflected from colored construction paper or light emitted from a computer screen as the light source (analytes and solvents at that time of reaction). A loss (OD) strategy is employed in performing the chemical analysis of metal ions to minimize the consumption of reagents (analyte and solvent) during metal ions monitoring. Therefore, compact devices have been developed to measure metal ion concentrations with low measurement levels. The redox reaction between metal ions and TA-AgNPs was evaluated by observing the color change with the naked eye at 0 and 60 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective identification of Cu(II) by TA-AgNPs on the surface of μPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S5 (see Supporting information) presents optical micrographs showing various concentrations of Cu(II) applied to the μPCDs surface. As detailed in Fig. S5, these optical images of the μPCDs were captured under natural light, assessing the color changes in TA-AgNPs traversing the μ-channels. These color shifts result from the disruption and aggregation of TA-AgNPs upon interaction with different Cu(II) ion concentrations. Results demonstrated that after 60 min in the presence of TA-AgNPs, Cu(II) appeared yellow in the detection zone of the μPCDs. To achieve this, 5 mL of TA-AgNPs was applied to each visible area, followed by 5 mL of the analytical solution. Distilled water was allocated to Zone 1, while Zone 2 received TA-AgNPs. Zones starting from Zone 3 were introduced to Cu(II) ions in concentrations ranging from 10 µM to 300 mM (Fig. S5(A, C (see Supporting information)) and see Video in the Supporting information). Notably, the sensing areas with higher Cu(II) ion concentrations (300 mM, 200 mM, 100 mM, 50 mM, 20 mM, 10 mM, 5 mM, and 1 mM) exhibited faster color change. These changes became evident after 60 min (see Fig. S5(B, D) and Supporting information Video). As incubation proceeded, nearly the entire range of Cu(II) concentrations adopted a yellow hue. Thus, observing color progression over time offers a semi-quantitative measure of Cu(II) concentration. Particularly, paper substrates yielded remarkable results. Linear analysis provided correlation coefficients of 0.9739 at the start and 0.9536 after 60 min of incubation. Visual color assessments were backed by Photo Metrix and Color Picker software to verify the effective movement of µPCDs, assuring reliable identification of Cu(II) ions in actual samples (Fig. S5(E&F) for supporting data). The absorption rate for analyte samples was calculated following Beer’s law.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective identification of Co(II) by TA-AgNPs on the surface of μPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S6 (see Supporting information) displays optical micrographs for assays detecting various concentrations of Co(II) on μPCDs. As depicted in Fig. S6, these optical images of μPCDs were taken in natural light, and they assess the color changes of TA-AgNPs (refer to Fig. S6 in Supporting information) within μ-channels using a capillary method. This change may result from the disruption of TA-AgNPs aggregation when interacting with varying levels of Co(II) ions. With the presence of TA-AgNPs, an orange hue appeared on the paper to indicate Co(II) after 60 min. For this purpose, 5 mL of TA-AgNPs was applied to each observable area, followed by the addition of 5 mL of the analytical solution. Zone 1 was reserved for DW, and Zone 2 specifically for TA-AgNPs. From Zone 3 onwards, the system was exposed to Co(II) ions at varying concentrations from 10 µM to 300 mM (Fig. S6(A, C) and Video in the ESI). Initially, a rapid color transformation was noted in the zones exposed to higher concentrations of Co(II) ions (300 mM, 200 mM, 100 mM, 50 mM, 20 mM, 10 mM). After 60 min, the resulting color changes were documented (Fig. S6(A&C)) and Video in the Supporting information). With continued incubation, nearly the full spectrum of Co(II) concentrations gradually turned orange. Thus, the progression of color over time can serve as an indicator for the semi-quantitative evaluation of Co(II) concentrations. Notably, significant results were attained using paper substrates. A linear examination of the data provided correlation coefficients of 0.9713 at the start of the reaction and 0.97 at 60 min. visual assessments of color changes by µPCDs, affirming reliable identification of Co(II) ions in real samples and user contentment (refer to Fig. S6(E&F) in the Supporting information). The absorption rate of the analyte samples was calculated following Beer's law.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective identification of Pb(II)by TA-AgNPs, using μPCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S7 (see Supporting information) reveals the optical micrographs for the different concentrations of Pb(II), detection assays performed on μPCDs. As illustrated in Fig. S7 (see in ESI†), the optical images on the μPCDs were captured under natural light, in which the color alternations of the TA-AgNPrs (Fig. S7) were evaluated through the μ-channels, via the capillary method. The reason might be due to the disruption of the TA-AgNPs aggregation when interfering with different concentrations of Pb(II) ion, In the presence of TA-AgNPs, after 0 min, it was revealed that Pb(II)appeared in indigo color on the paper. For this purpose, 5 mL of TA-AgNPs was deposited in each visible area, followed by the addition of 5 mL of the analytical solution. Zones 1 and 2 were designated for DW and TA-AgNPrs respectively. Meanwhile, from Zone 3 onwards, the system was subjected to Pb(II)ion at concentrations ranging from 10 µM to 300 mM (Fig. S7A (see Supporting information) and Video in the ESI†). The color transformation in the sensing zone exposed to lower and higher Pb(II)ion concentrations occurred in sensing zones. The color changes were observed after 0 min (Fig. S7B (see Supporting information)). Hence, for a semi-quantitative evaluation of Pb(II), the progression of color over time can be regarded as an indicator of Pb(II)concentration. Importantly, notable outcomes were achieved on paper substrates, Linear analysis of the data yielded correlation coefficient values of 0.922 in 0 min of reaction time. Visually assessed the color change by μPCDs for reliable Pb(II)ions identification in real samples, ensuring user satisfaction (Fig. S7 (see Supporting information). The analyte samples' absorption rate was determined per Beer's law. Therefore, it can be inferred that the application of the µPCDs-based colorimetric method has the potential to revolutionize ion discovery and present new opportunities in the field of ion detection in complex matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions in the biological sample

ce:section.ce:para: After successful detection of candidate ions by μPCDs, its application for determination of Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions assessed by colorimetric method. Initially, urine treatment was tested with the addition of 1 mM concentrations of Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions diluted in distilled water at a 1:1 vol ratio (Fig. 6 ). The primary analysis showed that the Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions manifested as an indigo color on the paper. However, further investigation revealed a chemical interaction that affected the concentration and absorbance of the metal ions, which did not correspond with the expected standard concentrations depicted in Fig. 6(A, B). Consequently, the TA-AgNPs shifted in color from blue to indigo when exposed to metal ions. This interaction between the metal ions and the nanoprobe environment could lead to the aggregation of TA-AgNP molecules, causing the observed color variation in the TA-AgNP solution with the presence of Cu(II), Co(II), and Pb(II) ions.

Table 1
[33–41] provides a summary of various analytical techniques for detecting metal ions up until now. In many cases, the analytical data reported meet or even surpass WHO standards. However, as far as we know, the majority of these advancements have yet to be converted into commercial diagnostic tools. The main reasons for industry reluctance to adopt these devices include: (I) complex fabrication, which requires sophisticated external components for sensor control and readout, inhibiting their independent or user-friendly usage; (II) low technological maturity in many instances, with limited reproducibility of device performance. Additionally, most reported efforts still rely on intricate instruments and toxic reagents for pre-treatments. To overcome these barriers, new fabrication techniques, updated detection systems, improved result display devices, and comprehensive evaluation protocols with real samples are essential [42–58]. Compared to prior studies (see Table 1), the results from this study highlight the developed method's advantages, including improved stability, optimal surface area, and effective biological activity.

To evaluate optical chemosensor for determining the target analyte, we used three different concentrations of metal ions (high, medium, and low) and performed colorimetric tests at different reaction times (20, 40 and 60 min). Fig. 7 indicating minimal changes in the three absorption bands. Although there was a gradual decrease in band intensity, the reduction ratios remained consistent for all bands without affecting their wavelength positions. These results validate the reliability of the tests, with the findings presented as relative standard deviation (RSD). [25] The provided data measurements of different concentrations of substances, which were assessed over a 60 min period using high, medium, and low concentrations following the initial measurement (Fig. 7). The relative standard deviation (RSD) within the data reflects the degree of change or dispersion in the measurements at each concentration level and time.
The standard deviation in various concentrations of Pb(II)ion is approximately 0.017 indicating high variability in the measurements around the mean central wavelength of 437 nm.
The standard deviation in various concentrations of Cu(II)ion is approximately 0.068 indicating moderate variability in measurements around the mean central wavelength of 437 nm.
The standard deviation in various concentrations of Co(II)ion is approximately 0.077 suggesting a similarly low level of variation around the mean wavelength of 437 nm.

TA-AgNPrs has been introduced as an innovative organic optical probe for sensitive detection and determination of metal ions in urine samples. An innovative optical chemosensor has been potentially suggested and evaluated for sensitive recognition of metal ions in urine samples, utilizing TA-AgNPs as an optical sensing element. The interaction between the metal ions molecule and TA-AgNPs leads to a substantial change in the absorption spectrum, enabling quick, reliable, and timely measurements with detection capabilities ranging from 1 nM to 300 mM. Consequently, it can be inferred that the application of the TA-AgNPs-based spectrophotometric method has the potential to revolutionize ion discovery and present new opportunities in the field of biomedical analysis. Furthermore, the designed optosensor allows the direct quantification of biological samples without the need for derivatization procedures or sample preparation. However, it is essential to acknowledge that certain factors-such as an individual's fluid intake, urine output, and ambient temperature-can affect urine concentration and should be regarded as important factors. Based on the obtained results, it is necessary to utilize some pre-concentration processes/methods to reach excellent analytical data on the routine spectrophotometric/colorimetric analysis of metal ions in real samples. But, utilization of μPCDs we overcome this limitation. According to obtained results by this platform, candidate ions (Cu(II), Pb(II), and Co(II) were detected by colorimetric assay in suitable resolution without need to pre-treatment process of analytes. Therefore, the designed optosensor allows the direct quantification of ions without the need for pre-concentration, derivatization or sample preparation procedures. One of the key advantages of this portable device is the suitable and accurate evaluation of metal ions concentration in human biological fluids using colorimetric method which opens new horizons for the early diagnosis of many diseases. Furthermore, μPCDs hold the potential for integration with smartphones, enabling personalized medicine. To the best of our knowledge, the proposed technique for selective metal ions detection in human urine samples, and on μPCDs is completely innovative and was not previously reported. It is anticipated that the utilization μPCDs can be adjusted to focus on liquid metal ions in the human biofluids.
Farnaz Bahavarnia: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohammad Hasanzadeh: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: All the authors (Farnaz Bahavarnia, Mohammad Hasanzadeh) have read, approved and made substantial contributions for the manuscript. None of the original material contained in this manuscript has been previously published nor is currently under review for publication elsewhere. Besides, authors declare no conflict of interests and/or commercial products or companies.‬‬
